# Grammar for people who aren't twats

## Transcript (auto-generated, lightly cleaned)

So, I had this idea for a book show called "Grammar for people who aren't twats." And I got this idea because I went to a talk by Gyles Brandreth who'd written a book called "Have You Grammar?" — which is apparently publishers want a book every 10 years on grammar, and it had been 10 years since *Eats, Shoots & Leaves*.

But what I realized was that there's this weird kind of class thing associated with grammar. It's like having to do your driving test in full dinner dress with a bow tie. And then there are these other weird bits of grammar — like split infinitives and the Oxford comma — which is like: that's not just requiring dinner dress. That's like you have to have a butler and a monocle.

In fact, there are just a very few simple things you need to learn for grammar. And everybody who's a speaker of English — not even a native speaker of English — is entitled to use it. It's not like there's some ivory tower of people who know how to use English.

The benefit of grammar — the real reason why anybody would want to use it — is so that you can reach more of those people who speak English. The estimates vary between about 1.5 billion and 2 billion people around the world speak this crazy language of ours. And so, if you can do things like get the apostrophes in the right place and figure out when to put a full stop to make a sentence, this really helps other people read it.

And the other thing I've realized is: it really helps you read it. When I've taken the trouble to properly punctuate my work and actually make the English more readable, when I read it, I realize where the holes are in my argument, what's wrong with it. It makes it much easier for me to read it and to think about it.

So, yeah — grammar shouldn't just be for twats.

## Key ideas

- Grammar is a class issue — the dinner dress / butler and monocle metaphor
- The real purpose of grammar is reach: 1.5–2 billion English speakers
- Proper punctuation helps the writer find holes in their own argument
- Grammar belongs to everyone who speaks English, not just an elite
